poem-1-1.note: Zhang Qian (c. 164–114 BC) — Han Dynasty diplomat and explorer who pioneered the Silk Road route to Central Asia on behalf of Emperor Wu.
Hujia — an ancient reed instrument of northern nomadic peoples, known for its desolate, tragic timbre, often used as a trope in frontier poetry.
Great Yuezhi — a nomadic people originally from the Hexi Corridor, later migrated to Central Asia. They were Zhang Qian's destination in seeking an alliance against the Xiongnu.
Han Staff (jié) — the imperial envoy's staff of authority, a bamboo rod topped with yak-tail tassels symbolizing the emperor's mandate and mission.

poem-1-2.note: Qilian Mountains — located at the border of Gansu and Qinghai, meaning 'Heavenly Mountain' in Xiongnu. It is the southern barrier of the Hexi Corridor and an ancient crucial passage to the Western Regions.

poem-1-3.note: Tangyi Fu — Zhang Qian's loyal Xiongnu companion and guide throughout his perilous journey to the Western Regions.

poem-1-5.note: Hexi Corridor — a narrow passage located in northwestern Gansu between the Qilian and Beishan mountains. It was the crucial throat connecting the Central Plains to the Western Regions and a key segment of the Silk Road.
Silk Road — the ancient network of trade routes connecting China to Central and West Asia, and Europe, named for the Chinese silk that was heavily traded westward.

poem-2-1.note: Huo Qubing (140–117 BC) — brilliant young Han general who defeated the Xiongnu at Qilian, securing the Hexi Corridor. He died prematurely at age 24.

poem-2-2.note: Dunhuang — an ancient oasis city at the westernmost end of the Hexi Corridor, a hub of the ancient Silk Road, world-renowned for its Mogao Caves.
Crescent Lake (Yueyaquan) — located at the foot of the Mingsha Mountains in Dunhuang, named for its crescent moon shape, historically praised as 'the first spring in the desert'.

poem-2-6.note: Jiuquan ('Wine Spring') — a city in Gansu named after a legend that General Huo Qubing poured imperial wine into a spring to share it with all his troops.

poem-3-1.note: Künes — an alpine meadow in the middle section of the Tianshan Mountains (Xinyuan County, Ili, Xinjiang), rich in water and grass, the core pasture of the ancient Wusun State.
Liu Xijun — daughter of Liu Jian, King of Jiangdu in the Western Han. In 105 BC, Emperor Wu sent her to marry the Wusun King (Kunmo) in a political alliance.
Wusun — an ancient nomadic kingdom located in the Ili River valley (modern Kazakhstan/Xinjiang) that allied with the Han Dynasty through marriage diplomacy.

poem-3-2.note: Ili River — major river flowing from the Tianshan Mountains into Lake Balkhash, historically the cradle of Wusun and Kazakh cultures.

poem-3-5.note: Song of Sorrow — composed by Princess Xijun, expressing deep lament over being married off to an alien, distant land. It is the earliest recorded 'Heqin' (marriage alliance) poem in Chinese literary history.
Yellow Swan — referenced in Princess Xijun's Song of Sorrow, reading 'I wish I were a yellow swan, returning to my hometown.' It has since become a classic literary trope for homesickness.
Kizil Ardak — meaning 'red flower' in Kazakh, referring to the wild tulips common in the Ili River Valley of Xinjiang that bloom fiery red in spring.

poem-4-1.note: Li Guangli — Western Han general commissioned by Emperor Wu, who led two immense expeditions to Dayuan (104–101 BC) to obtain the famed 'blood-sweating' horses.
Blood-Sweating Horses — the legendary Akhal-Teke horses of Fergana. Their skin was said to 'sweat blood' during intense exertion.
Dayuan (Fergana) — an ancient kingdom in the Fergana Valley of Central Asia, famous for breeding the high-quality 'blood-sweating' Akhal-Teke horses.
Pamir Mountains — located in southeastern Central Asia, historically known as the 'Onion Range', it was the highest barrier crossed by the ancient Silk Road connecting China and Central Asia.

poem-4-3.note: Taklamakan Desert — China's largest desert, located in the Tarim Basin of Xinjiang. In Uyghur, it allegedly means 'place from which there is no return', spanning around 330,000 square kilometers.
Uzbel Pass — an important mountain pass on the Pamir Plateau, located on the border between China and Kyrgyzstan, it was a western exit of the ancient Silk Road.
Amu Darya — the longest river in Central Asia, historically Guishui. The Great Yuezhi settled in the Amu Darya basin after migrating westward.

poem-4-4.note: Emperor Wu (Liu Che, 156–87 BC) — the seventh emperor of the Western Han. A visionary ruler who expanded the empire westward and dispatched Zhang Qian to open the Silk Road.

poem-4-4.term: Song of the Heavenly Horses from the Western Extremity
poem-4-4.note: Song of the Heavenly Horses — a Yuefu poem composed personally by Emperor Wu of Han after obtaining the blood-sweating horses, praising their majestic spirit to express his grand imperial ambitions.

poem-5-1.note: Lop Nur — once a vast salt lake in eastern Xinjiang, now a completely dry lake bed. It was the site of the ancient Loulan ruins and later China's nuclear testing ground.

Desert Poplar (Populus euphratica) — a highly drought and salt-resistant deciduous tree found around the Taklamakan Desert. It is said to 'live a thousand years, stand dead for a thousand years, and remain un-rotted for a thousand years'.
Tamarisk (Red Willow) — a common shrub or small tree in northwestern deserts, highly resistant to salt and drought, serving as a crucial plant for windbreaks and sand fixation.
Loulan — an ancient Silk Road kingdom near Lop Nur that mysteriously vanished around the 4th century AD primarily due to desertification and changing river courses.

poem-6-1.note: Lake Baikal — the deepest and oldest freshwater lake in the world, located in Siberia. It is the historical "North Sea" where Han envoy Su Wu was exiled to tend sheep.
Su Wu — a Han Dynasty envoy (c. 140–60 BC) who was held captive by the Xiongnu for 19 years at Lake Baikal, famously clutching his Han imperial staff and refusing to surrender.

poem-6-3.note: Li Ling — grandson of the famous Han general Li Guang. He surrendered to the Xiongnu after fighting heroically until his arrows ran out, a surrender that remained deeply controversial.

poem-6-4.note: Swan Goose — in legend, a Han envoy bluffed the Xiongnu by claiming the Emperor shot down a goose with a letter from Su Wu tied to its leg, securing Su Wu's release. Thus 'swan goose' became a metaphor for postal letters.

poem-7-1.note: Yarkand — an ancient Western Regions location, present-day Shache County in Xinjiang. The Yarkand River is one of the main tributaries of the Tarim River.

Silk Princess — a legendary Central Plains princess who hid silkworm eggs in her royal headdress when married off to the King of Khotan, thus smuggling sericulture technology into the Western Regions.

Yangguan Pass — a Han Dynasty border fortress southwest of Dunhuang, an essential checkpoint on the southern route of the Silk Road. Made famous by Wang Wei's poem 'West of Yangguan is no old friend'.

poem-7-5.note: Asikhan — the legendary King of Khotan (Yutian) who married the Silk Princess that smuggled the silkworm seeds from the Central Plains.

poem-8-1.note: Ban Chao (32–102 AD) — a legendary Eastern Han general and diplomat who, with only 36 men, re-established Chinese suzerainty across the Western Regions over a 31-year campaign.
Shanshan — a Silk Road kingdom (formerly Loulan); the site of Ban Chao's daring night raid on a Xiongnu delegation, his first major victory in the Western Regions.

poem-9-1.note: Gan Ying — a Han military envoy dispatched by Ban Chao in 97 AD to establish contact with Da Qin (the Roman Empire). He reached the Persian Gulf but was dissuaded from crossing.

poem-9-3.note: Zagros Mountains — a major mountain range in western Iran, stretching from the Persian Gulf to eastern Turkey, acting as a natural barrier on the Silk Road to Mesopotamia.
Persian Gulf — an arm of the Arabian Sea between the Arabian Peninsula and southwestern Iran. Gan Ying purportedly reached its shores before abandoning his voyage to Rome.

poem-10-1.note: Tarim River — China's longest inland river, stretching about 2,137 kilometers along the northern edge of the Tarim Basin. Historically, the lifeblood of garrison agriculture in the Western Regions.

Suo Mai — an Eastern Han general who led 3,000 troops from Dunhuang to forcibly cross the Tarim River, pioneering large-scale military agricultural colonies (tuntian) in the heart of the Western Regions.

Military Reclamation — the system of using military troops to cultivate wasteland for agricultural production. Originating in the Han Dynasty's 'tuntian' policy in the Western Regions, it is the historical precedent for the Xinjiang Production and Construction Corps.

poem-11-1.note: Mendicant Monk — referencing the Tang Dynasty monk Xuanzang (602–664). Because he left China illegally in defiance of an imperial travel ban to seek Buddhist scriptures, he was, initially, a stowaway mendicant.

poem-11-6.note: Suyab — one of the ancient Four Garrisons of Anxi, located near modern Tokmok in Kyrgyzstan. A crucial Tang Dynasty fortress town and famously the purported birthplace of poet Li Bai.
Issyk-Kul — a vast endorheic lake in the northern Tian Shan mountains in eastern Kyrgyzstan, the second-largest alpine lake in the world.
Yabghu Qaghan — ruler of the Western Turkic Khaganate who warmly received Xuanzang in Central Asia, providing him with escorts and diplomatic credentials to continue his westward journey.
Xuanzang (602–664) — a legendary Tang Dynasty Buddhist monk whose 17-year overland pilgrimage to India and meticulous geographic records inspired the classic novel 'Journey to the West'.

poem-12-1.note: Cen Shen (c. 715–770) — one of the greatest Tang Dynasty frontier poets, celebrated for his majestic, harsh, and vivid depictions of Silk Road landscapes and military campaigns.
